lesson__text: React & Next.js are taking over! I will guide you step-by-step through the process of building modern website & web applications with React.js and Next.js.

Learn why & when to use the useEffect hook. The useEffect hook is used for interacting with things outside React (e.g. window / document object for attaching event listeners, servers for fetching data, etc.). It's the most complicated hook, so it's OK if you don't understand it at this point.

lesson__title: The problem with the Context API
lesson__text: The Context API has 1 big downside: no selectors. This means that if anything in the context value changes, all components that use that context value will re-render, even if they don't use the value that changed. This is not good for performance. We can solve this with Zustand or Redux.

Learn the fundamentals of authentication on the web.
A JWT is stored in a cookie. A cookie is automatically sent with every request to the server. This is how we can keep a user logged in.
A session is the period of time a user is logged in. We can associate information with a session, such as the user's id, email, subscription status, etc.
The cookie is automatically sent with every request to the server. The Authorization header is not. We can use either for authentication.
The JWT is a string with three parts separated by dots. The first part is the header, the second part is the payload, and the third part is the signature.
People generally perceive JWT to be better for performance and scalability. Database sessions are generally perceived to be better for security.
For static assets like images you typically use a CDN. For computing like checking if a user is logged in, you can now also use something like a CDN (called 'edge computing' or 'edge network').
I generally prefer a third-party auth solution, but Next-Auth is what we'll use here.
Middleware is a function that runs before the request is handled by the server. Middleware can be used for authentication, logging, etc.
